paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - SYMMETRY DRIVEN GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Exploiting symmetries and invariance in data is a powerful, yet not fully exploited, way to achieve better generalisation with more efficiency. In this paper, we introduce two graph network architectures that are equivariant to several types of transformations affecting the node coordinates. First, we build equivariance to any transformation in the coordinate embeddings that preserves the distance between neighbouring nodes, allowing for equivariance to the Euclidean group. Then, we introduce angle attributes to build equivariance to any angle preserving transformation - thus, to the conformal group. Thanks to their equivariance properties, the proposed models can be vastly more data efficient with respect to classical graph architectures, intrinsically equipped with a better inductive bias and better at generalising. We demonstrate these capabilities on a synthetic dataset composed of _n_ -dimensional geometric objects. Additionally, we provide examples of their limitations when (the right) symmetries are not present in the data.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Symmetries exist throughout nature. All the fundamental laws of physics are built upon the framework of symmetries, from the gauge groups describing the Standard Model of particle physics, to Einstein’s theories of general and special relativity. Once one understands the symmetry of a certain system, powerful predictions can be made. A notable example is that of Gell-Mann’s eightfoldway (Gell-Mann, 1961), built upon the symmetries observed in hadrons, that led to his prediction of the Ω _[−]_ baryon, which was subsequently observed 3 years later (Barnes et al., 1964). The study of symmetries and invariance in deep learning has recently become a field of interest to the community (see, _e.g._ , (Bronstein et al., 2021) for a comprehensive overview), and rapid progress has been made in constructing architectures with group theoretic structures embedded within. Two fundamental architectures in machine learning, the convolutional and graph neural networks, are invariant to the translation and permutation groups respectively.

Graph networks in particular are designed to learn from graph-structured data and are by construction invariant to permutations of the input nodes. They were originally proposed in (Gori et al., 2005; Scarselli et al., 2008) and have received a lot of attention in the last years (see, _e.g._ , (Battaglia et al., 2018; Hamilton, 2020; Wu et al., 2020) for a comprehensive overview). Due to their properties, they find application in a broad range of problems like learning the dynamics of complex physical systems (Sanchez-Gonzalez et al., 2020; Pfaff et al., 2021), particle identification in particle physics (Dreyer & Qu, 2021), learning causal and relational graphs (Kipf et al., 2018; Li et al., 2020), discovering symbolic models (Cranmer et al., 2020), as well as quantum chemistry (Gilmer et al., 2017) and drug discovery (Stokes et al., 2020).

In this work, we decouple node coordinates from other node attributes to obtain invariance (and possibly equivariance) to many transformations affecting the node coordinates, possibly belonging to important groups, in addition to permutation invariance. First, we define the _distance preserving graph network_ (DGN) whose updated node, edge and global attributes are invariant to any transformation in the coordinate embeddings that preserves the distance between neighbouring nodes, while node coordinates can be updated in an equivariant way. Examples of such transformations are rotations and translations, but also transformations on the Hoberman sphere, whose 3D shape changes while preserving distances between connected nodes. Equivariance to dilations in the coordinates can be obtained via the conformal orthogonal group with the inclusion of an additional input layer. Then, we define the _angle preserving graph network_ (AGN), whose updated node, edge and global attributes (resp. node coordinates) are invariant (resp. equivariant) to any transformation preserving

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

the angles between triples of neighbouring nodes in the graph (a notable example being molecular conformations) and, thus, to the _n_ -dimensional conformal group on R _[n]_ .

By constructing such architectures, we enable a wide range of possible transformations to be performed on graph-structured data, with the only requirement being that distances or angles between coordinates of neighbouring nodes are preserved. This means that the updated attributes/coordinates of both networks are invariant/equivariant to translations, rotations, reflections ( _i.e._ , the Euclidean group, _E_ ( _n_ )), with the AGN allowing also invariance/equivariance to dilations, inversions and nonorthogonal rotations. In practice, this means the networks are able to filter out many copies of the same input whose coordinate embeddings have been transformed and therefore learn more efficiently than architectures which consider the inputs as distinct. In other words, a single sample contains the same information as many copies of it obtained by appropriately transforming it.

Finally, while the two architectures we present are _partially_ overlapping in terms of symmetries, it is important to consider specific use cases where the use of one architecture over another would be preferred. We test the architectures on a synthetic dataset consisting of _n_ -dimensional geometric shapes and other benchmark datasets. Moreover, we show examples of cases in which using our architectures is counterproductive due to the lack of symmetries in the data.

## 2 BACKGROUND

## 2.1 GROUP THEORY AND EQUIVARIANCE

Group theory provides the mathematical formulation for symmetries of systems, with symmetry operations represented by individual group elements. A group can be defined as a set _G_ equipped with a binary operation, which enables one to combine two group elements to form a third, whilst preserving the group axioms (associativity, identity, closure and inverse). A function unaffected by a group action is said to be _invariant_ , which is particular instance of _equivariance_ . Formally, let _X ⊆_ R _[n]_ and _ϕg_ : _X → X_ be a transformation on _X_ for a group element _g ∈ G_ . Then, the linear map Φ : _X → Y_ , _Y ⊆_ R _[n]_ , is equivariant to _G_ if _∀g ∈ G_ , _∃ϕ[′] g_[:] _[ Y][→][Y]_[such that]

When _ϕ[′] g_[is the identity, we say that][ Φ][ is invariant to] _[ G]_[.][The functional form of][ Φ][ is determined by] the specific group of interest. It is worth pointing out that, whilst not often expressed in group theoretic notation, equivariance exists in common deep learning architectures; the convolution operation used in CNNs is equivariant under the translation group and approximate invariance is typically achieved via pooling operations. Graph neural networks are invariant to the permutation group.

**Euclidean group** The translation and orthogonal groups ( _T_ ( _n_ ) and _O_ ( _n_ ) respectively) act through translations, orthogonal rotations and reflections. The semidirect product of these two groups _E_ ( _n_ ) = _T_ ( _n_ ) ⋊ _O_ ( _n_ ) is known as the Euclidean group, which consists of transformations that preserves distances. A function that is invariant under _E_ ( _n_ ) is _∥_ **x** _i −_ **x** _j∥_ 2[2][, for any] **[ x]** _[i][,]_ **[ x]** _[j][∈]_[E] _[n]_[ (see] Appendix A for a proof).

**Conformal group** The conformal group Conf(R _[n,]_[0] ) is the group of transformations _ϕ_ : R _[n] →_ R _[n]_ that preserve angles. Formally, for any triple of vectors **x** _j,_ **x** _i,_ **x** _k ∈_ R _[n]_ , let us denote by ∠( **x** _j,_ **x** _i,_ **x** _k_ ) the angle centred on **x** _i_ with rays given by **x** _j −_ **x** _i_ and **x** _k −_ **x** _i_ . Then, a conformal transformation _ϕ_ satisfies

By definition, the transformations of the conformal group include translations, rotations, reflections (which collectively form the Euclidean group _E_ ( _n_ ), as well as dilations, inversions and other features. An important subgroup is the conformal orthogonal group, which requires transformations to be orthogonal (and thus, inversions are not allowed).

The simplest map satisfying the above definitions is the identity function

which keeps the coordinates unchanged during the update step. Updating all the coordinates in the same way also trivially preserves both relative distances and angles. In the case where the coordinate embedding _X_ is a Conformal orthogonal transformation of _Y_ a possible map _ψ_ is defined by

## 3 EQUIVARIANT GRAPH NETWORKS

We start this section by recalling the structure of a standard graph network block. Then, we introduce two novel architectures with additional invariance and equivariance properties with respect to different node coordinate embeddings. We stress that while we build our discussion upon equivariance with respect to node coordinates (since there is, in general, no sense of equivariance of the network’s node or edge embeddings), there may be cases in which our discussion can be extended to node or edge embeddings.

## 3.1 THE STARTING POINT - GRAPH NETWORK BLOCK (GN)

A general standard graph network block can be defined in terms of edge, node and global updates as

where _φ[e]_ : R[2] _[n][v]_[+] _[n][e]_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] e_[+] , _φ[v]_ : R _[n][v]_[+] _[n] e_[+][+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] v_[+] , _φ[u]_ : R _[n] v_[+][+] _[n]_[+] _e_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] u_[+] are update functions (usually defined as neural networks whose parameters are to be learned) and _ρ[e][→][v] , ρ[e][→][u] , ρ[v][→][u]_ are aggregation functions reducing a set of elements of variable length to a single one via an input’s permutation equivariant operation like element-wise summation or mean.
3.2 DISTANCE PRESERVING GRAPH NETWORK BLOCK (DGN) By decoupling the updates of node coordinates and features ( **x** _i_ and **h** _i_ ), the DGN block is defined through the following updates

Because of the way in which node coordinates are processed to update edge and global embeddings ( _i.e._ , only through their relative distances), it can be easily seen that **e**[+] _ji_[,] **[h]**[+] _i_[and] **[u]**[+][are,][by][con-] struction, invariant to any transformation of the input coordinates that locally maintains their relative distances along the edges defined by the graph structure (a notable example being the one of Euclidean transformations; see Appendix D for a proof). Moreover, updated node coordinates **x**[+] _i_[can] be invariant or equivariant to (some of) those transformations, depending on the particular structure of _ψ[x]_ . For example, using equation 3 or the trivial identity function (equation 2) would result in coordinates being updated in an equivariant way with respect to Euclidean transformations, with _aji_ possibly being parametric functions ( _e.g._ , _aji_ = _φ[x]_ ( **e** _ji_ )).

## 3.3 ANGLE PRESERVING GRAPH NETWORK BLOCK (AGN)

Given a graph _G_ ( _V, E_ ), let _**α** jik ∈_ R _[n][α]_ be the angle embedding associated to each angle ( _j, i, k_ ) _∈ A_ and assume that _**α** jik_ contain no information about the angles ∠( **x** _j,_ **x** _i,_ **x** _k_ ). Moreover, define _Ai_

as the set of (ordered) couples of nodes forming an angle whose vertex is node _i_ , _i.e._ , _Ai_ ≜ _{_ ( _j, k_ ) _|_ ( _j, y, k_ ) _∈A, y_ = _i}_ . Then, the AGN block is then characterised by the following updates

where _φ[α] , φ[e] , φ[h] , ψ[x] , φ[u]_ are the update functions[1] , and _ρ[e][→][h] , ρ[α][→][h] , ρ[h][→][u] , ρ[e][→][u] , ρ[α][→][u]_ are the aggregation functions, with _ψ[x]_ being a (possibly parametric) _relative angle preserving map_ . Variants of this network that still preserve its properties can be easily constructed by, _e.g._ , using edge embeddings to update the angle embeddings, using angles to update edges or not considering angle attributes at all. Some examples are reported in Appendix F.1. It can be easily seen that _**α**_[+] _jik[,]_ **[ e]** _ji_[+] _[,]_ **[ h]** _i_[+] _[,]_ **[ u]**[+][are][invariant][to][any][transformation][of][the][coordinate][embeddings][that][preserves] the angles created by neighbouring nodes in the graph, while updated coordinates **x**[+] _i_[can be invari-] ant or equivariant to (some of) the same transformations, depending on the structure of _ψ[x]_ . As with the DGN, equivariance to the conformal orthogonal group can be achieved by using equation 3. A particular case of transformations the AGN can deal with is the one of conformal transfomations (see Appendix E for a formal discussion).

## 4 DISCUSSION

In the previous section we introduced two novel graph architectures. To construct them we have mainly built upon the actions on a coordinate system of two transformations that preserves distances and angles between neighbouring nodes in a graph. These transformations include as particular cases those in the Euclidean and conformal groups. Equivariance under _E_ ( _n_ ) means equivariance to orthogonal rotations and translations, while by considering the full conformal group, we can further generalise our neural network architecture. Conformal _n_ -dimensional transformations consist of the groups containing translations, dilations, rotations and inversions with respect to an _n −_ 1 sphere. A conformal transformation is therefore a powerful tool for mapping data points onto each other, and hence, building a neural network architecture invariant to the conformal group enables the architecture to be invariant to a wide selection of interesting subgroups. By introducing distance and angle preserving transformations that take into account the structure of the graph, invariance and equivariance under more general transformations have been achieved. A notable example is that the DGN architecture, whose updates are invariant to any transformation of a Hoberman sphere, while both the DGN and AGN can be invariant to transformations affecting only subsets of their nodes, as shown below.

In summary, by taking advantage of the powerful tools of group theory, and performing operations on a vector space which are invariant or equivariant under group transformations, we can build a neural network architecture which can take advantage of these group properties. Such an architecture will be able to deal with rotated, translated, dilated (or more generally transformed) data more efficiently than a standard graph network which does not have the above group properties built into it.

## 4.1 BEYOND GLOBAL TRANSFORMATIONS

So far, we have talked about global transformations in the coordinate space. In general, a global symmetry _ϕg_ , as defined in Section 2.1 acts on a function _ψ_ ( **x** ) as

> 1The update functions in equation 6 live in the function spaces _φα_ : R3 _nh_ + _nα_ +1+ _nu →_ R _n_[+] _α_ , _φ[e]_ : R[2] _[n][h]_[+] _[n][e]_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] e_[+] , _φ[h]_ : R _[n][h]_[+] _[n] e_[+][+] _[n]_[+] _α_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] h_[+] , _ψ[x]_ : R _[K] →_ R _[n][x]_ , for an appropriate _K ∈_ Z[+] depending on the actual number of parameters, and _φ[u]_ : R _[n] h_[+][+] _[n] e_[+][+] _[n]_[+] _α_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] u_[+] .

Figure 3: Equivariance of the two networks. The green/red square denotes the base graph and the blue/black ones alternative coordinate embeddings, equivariant for the DGN (red) or AGN (green).

where now the group action can differ at all points.

The architectures we present are also able to deal with some local symmetries. Namely, the DGN and AGN preserve local distances and angles respectively _where a suitable subgraph must be defined_ . As an example consider a transformation that rotates only some nodes of a graph; all nodes which are in the neighbourhoods of the nodes will, in the angular architecture, have their angles preserved, and in the distance-preserving architecture, the distances between nodes will be preserved. A pertinent use-case for such a symmetry is that of molecular conformations, where the spatial arrangement of the atoms can be interconverted by rotations about formally single bonds). Both the DGN and the AGN updates would be invariant to such a transformation at the level of the two distinct subgraphs separated by the bond in question, thus enabling one to learn conformation-invariant properties.

## 4.2 COMPARING AND EXTENDING THE TWO ARCHITECTURES

As mentioned, the two architectures we presented are partially overlapping in terms of equivariance features. Whilst both of them contain invariance to _E_ ( _n_ ), the DGN can deal with non-orthogonal transformations that preserve the distance between neighbouring nodes (such as with the Hoberman sphere) while the AGN is invariant to the conformal group which includes non-orthogonal transformations that preserve angles but not distances (see Figure 3).

The DGN can be equipped with a sense of scale invariance through an input scaling layer that makes it invariant to the conformal orthogonal group. Let _γ_ = _α/_ max( _i,j_ ) _∈E ∥_ **x** _i −_ **x** _j∥_ for some _α ∈_ R, where we identify _γ_ as satisfying the dilation condition required under the conformal orthogonal group. Then, conformal orthogonal invariance can simply be obtained by computing scale-˜ normalised coordinates **x** _i_ = _γ_ **x** _i_ and using them as the coordinates in equation 5.

Introducing coordinate scaling therefore enables the DGN to be scale invariant, under the condition that all transformations remain orthogonal. With the AGN, we can relax this condition and be invariant under the full conformal group. This larger group invariance enables us to work with very powerful transformations on data, which can also be dangerous. For example, if the scale is an important property of the dataset, the AGN will not recognise it and so will learn incorrect information about the data. Similarly, one must also not use the DGN blindly and irrespective of the properties of the dataset; as the architecture is invariant to transformations that preserve distance between neighbouring nodes but not angles it is able to, for example, map a square onto a line. The two architectures we presented can be also combined together at the price of losing some features, while generalising to both distance and angle preserving transformations.

## 5 RELATED WORK

The study and formulation of group equivariant neural networks have flourished in the last years and they have proven to be extremely powerful in many tasks. The first example is probably that of convolutional neural networks (LeCun et al., 1990), which explots translation equivariance and invariance, thanks to the convolution and pooling operations respectively, and have led to breakthroughs in most vision tasks. CNNs have been generalised to exploit larger groups of symmetries. G-CNNs are proposed in (Cohen & Welling, 2016) to deal with more general symmetries, including rotations and translations, and extended in (Bekkers, 2020; Finzi et al., 2020) to deal with Lie groups. Equivariance to arbitrary symmetry groups can be achieved via self-attention mechanisms in (Romero & Cordonnier, 2021). Continuous convolutions are used in SchNet (Sch¨utt et al., 2017)

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

to achieve _E_ ( _n_ ) invariance, and _SE_ (3) equivariance is achieved in Thomas et al. (2018) via the use of spherical harmonics. The drawback of many of these methods is their limited applicability due to computational complexity.

There has been some work on constructing general MLPs that are equivariant to different groups; a layer equivariant to general matrix groups is presented in Finzi et al. (2021), whilst equivariance to the Lorentz group for physics applications has recently been explored (Bogatskiy et al., 2020). Further applications include (Mattheakis et al., 2019), where physical symmetries are embedded in neural networks via embedding physical constraints in the structure of the network, and in (Barenboim et al., 2021) where the ability of neural networks to discover and learn symmetries in data is explored.

Graph neural networks are, by construction, permutation invariant (Scarselli et al., 2008; Battaglia et al., 2018). Recently, there has been a lot of work on building equivariance to other interesting groups in GNNs. There has been particular interest in the Euclidean group with results for the subgroups _SE_ (3) and _E_ (3) obtained in (Thomas et al., 2018; Fuchs et al., 2020; K¨ohler et al., 2020; Finzi et al., 2020; Batzner et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020). Extending these architectures, a messagepassing convolutional graph layer is proposed in (Satorras et al., 2021) which implements _E_ ( _n_ ) invariance for edge and node features and equivariance for node coordinates, leading to state of the art results in a variety of tasks, including _n_ -body particle systems simulations and molecular property predictions. A similar idea has been also proposed in (Farina & Slade, 2021), where a general _E_ ( _n_ ) equivariant GNN is presented, and in (Horie et al., 2021), in which equivariance to isometric transformations is considered. These _E_ ( _n_ ) equivariant networks can be seen as special instances of our DGN architecture, where particular aggregation functions are applied and a particular choice is made for the distance preserving map _ψ[x]_ (see Appendix G). In (Satorras et al., 2021), for example, the map _ψ[x]_ is chosen so that relative distances between all possible pairs of node in the graph are preserved, thus allowing only for rigid transformations in _E_ ( _n_ ).

Angular information is used in (Smith et al., 2017) but no attention is explicitly paid to equivariance. DimeNET is proposed in (Klicpera et al., 2020), where both distance and angle embeddings computed through embeddings in novel orthogonal basis functions are used in a message passing graph network to achieve equivariance. State of the art results are obtained on molecular property and dynamics prediction datasets. We note, however, that while DimeNET produces state of the art results, it is restricted to atomic data, due to the requirement of Gaussian radial and Bessel functions as the basis representations. Additionally, angular embeddings computed in the isometric invariant layer in (Horie et al., 2021) corresponds to the extraction of both relative distances and angles of each pair of vertices. Again, these approaches are special instances of our networks where particular aggregation and learnable functions or hand engineered distance and/or angle embeddings are used (see Appendix G). In this sense, our network is unconstrained and can learn what it needs. Moreover, thanks to its general form it can achieve invariance to the conformal group (and more) which, to the best of our knowledge, does not hold for known architectures.

In summary, most of the existing equivariant graph architectures are at most equivariant to the Euclidean group (represented as the intersection set in Figure 3), whilst the architectures we propose allow, in their general form, more general transformations.

## 6 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

In this section we give an insight into the performance of the architectures we proposed and on their limitations. First, we demonstrate that perfect generalisation to unseen data can be achieved on datasets with a large number of symmetries, possibly accompanied by a faster convergence rate (as shown for QM9 in Appendix H.4). Then, we show that, when used on datasets with few symmetries, using our architectures can be counterproductive. In general, when the right symmetries are present, equivariant architectures experience both increased accuracy and convergence speed, as demonstrated by state of the art results obtained on a number of tasks by architectures that can be obtained as particular instances of the ones proposed here. Examples include _n_ -body system dynamics prediction, molecular dynamics and molecular property prediction tasks (Satorras et al., 2021; Klicpera et al., 2020; Horie et al., 2021).

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 6.1 POLYTOPES CLASSIFICATION

We consider a _n_ -dimensional polytopes classification problems for _n_ = 3 _,_ 4 _,_ 5. The datasets are composed of graph representations of regular polytopes and the number of classes varies with _n_ . Simplexes, hypercubes and orthoplexes are considered for all values of _n_ . For _n_ = 3 also dodecahedra and icosahedra are added, while for _n_ = 4 we consider 24 _−_ , 120 _−_ and 600 _−_ cell polytopes. Additional results are reported in Appendix H.2. The training dataset consists of a single graph per polytope where the graph is specified in terms of node coordinates **x** _i_ (so that the resulting polytope is inscribed in a hypersphere of radius 1) and list of edges _E_ (and possibly angles _A_ ); node, edge and possible angle attributes ( **h** _i,_ **e** _ji,_ _**α** jik_ ) are fixed to be equal to 0 for every node in the graph. This makes the problem intrinsically harder since only coordinates can be used to discern between polytopes. The test set is composed of randomly transformed versions of those in the training set,˜ whose coordinates are obtained as **x** _i_ = _γA_ **x** _i_ + _q_ for some _γ ∈_ R, _A ∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ and _q ∈_ R _[n]_ . Note that, while even a standard GN could learn to correctly classify transformed polytopes, a much larger training dataset is needed in order to do so, as shown in Appendix H.

We compare graph networks built with AGN, DGN and standard (GN) blocks, as well as EGNN (Satorras et al., 2021), DimeNET (Klicpera et al., 2020) and SE3-Transformer (Fuchs et al., 2020), the latter only for _n_ = 3. The DGN block is also combined with a scaling layer (SDGN) as in Section 4.2 to obtain equivariance to dilations. Architectures based on AGN, DGN and GN blocks employ sum aggregation functions _ρ_ and _ψ[x]_ set to equation 3 (other cases are considered in the appendix). Results are reported in Table 1.
Table 1: Polytopes classification: training and test accuracy (mean _±_ standard deviation over 10 runs) for different transformations in the test set.

We note that all models reach a perfect accuracy on the training set. As for the test accuracy, results vary across the different models, in line with what one expects from their invariance properties. The standard GN cannot generalise to any type of transformation. When only orthogonal transformations are performed in the test set ( _i.e._ , _A[⊤] A_ = _I_ , _γ_ = 1) all models (except GN) perfectly generalise to unseen data. However, as expected, when also adding dilations ( _i.e._ , _A[⊤] A_ = _γI_ , _γ ∈_ R), only AGN and SDGN (and the SE3-Transformer for _n_ = 3) can generalise well. Finally, when adding random non-orthogonal transformations ( _i.e._ , _µ_ = E[ _∥A[⊤] A − I∥F_ ] _>_ 0, _γ ∈_ R), something possibly unexpected occurs. The AGN (and partially also the SDGN and the SE3-Transformer for _n_ = 3) perfectly generalises to unseen polytopes whose neither angles nor edge lengths have been preserved (due to the non-orthogonality of _A_ ). This is probably due to the networks having learnt to distinguish polytopes based on the sum (or mean) of their angles which is preserved under nonorthogonal transformations due to the Gram–Euler theorem. While this behaviour turns out to be useful in the task at hand, one can easily envisage cases in which it may cause issues.

## 6.2 BENCHMARK DATASETS

Now we consider benchmark datasets from (Dwivedi et al., 2020) for which node coordinates are provided. In particular, we consider MNIST, CIFAR10 as graph classification problems and TSP as an edge classification one. When treated as images, MNIST and CIFAR10 are usually examples of datasets containing symmetries. However, when represented as graphs as in (Dwivedi et al., 2020),

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

those symmetries are mostly lost. This is due to, for example, the fact that both the foreground and the background of the images are embedded in the graphs. Furthermore, in TSP there are no apparent symmetries. In these cases, as one may expect, using an equivariant network would not produce any benefits. Rather, as we show, negative effects can appear.

We trained our models and a standard GNN on these datasets. All models have roughly the same number of parameters and the results are reported in Table 2. As can be seen, treating node coordinates as if they can posses some symmetric between among different samples turns out to be counterproductive both in terms of convergence speed and accuracy. In particular we see that while the standard GN has the best performance, the DGN is slightly better than the AGN. This is probably due to the fact that, while some non-orthogonal transformations mostly preserving local distances may be present in the dataset, angle preserving ones are much more rare. On TSP in particular, using angle related properties results in a significant drop in performance.
Table 2: Benchmark datasets. Train and test accuracy are reported for MNIST and CIFAR10. For TSP, due to the high class unbalance, the train and test F1 score for the positive class is reported.

## 6.2.1 COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY

All the experiments have been run on an NVIDIA V100 GPU. The average time required for each training step ( _i.e._ , forward pass, back-propagation and optimiser step for a batch of data) is reported in Table 3 for the architectures presented in this paper, normalised with respect to the batch-size. For the polytopes classification experiment we report the results obtained by using equation 2 and equation 3 (in parentheses). Using the scaling layer for the DGN drastically increase the computational complexity. Moreover, it can be seen that while the AGN and DGN have similar complexities for dataset in which the number of edges (and hence angles) is relatively low, when graphs are (nearly) complete (like in TSP) or highly connected, the AGN has an overhead with respect to the DGN (due to the need of updated and computing angles in addition to edges).

## 7 CONCLUSION

In this paper we have presented novel deep learning architectures which are equivariant to distance and angle preserving transformations in graph coordinate embeddings. In particular, we have shown invariance or equivariance to the _E_ ( _n_ ), CO(R _[n] , Q_ ) and Conf(R _[n,]_[0] ) groups in addition to permutation invariance. Invariance to local symmetries can also be achieved when the different transformations are applied to suitable subgraphs. We have applied our models to a synthetic dataset composed of _n_ -dimensional regular polytopes as well as to several benchmark datasets. We have shown that the architectures we propose are significantly more accurate and data efficient than a standard graph network on datasets where there are large numbers of symmetries in the data. We also explicitly show examples where our architecture produces unexpected results or would not be applicable, due to a lack of symmetry in the data.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting framework for symmetry-driven GNNs (DGN and AGN) that extend typical Euclidean equivariance to include conformal symmetries, allowing for invariance to dilations and non-orthogonal transformations. The conceptual bridging of graph structures with distance/angle preserving maps is well-motivated, and the distinction between global and local symmetries (like molecular conformations) is a strong point. However, there are notable technical ambiguities regarding the construction of 'angle embeddings' and the implementation of the map ψ, as well as some confusion regarding the claims of perfect generalization to non-orthogonal transformations on the polytopes dataset, which may stem from unintended feature leakage via the Gram-Euler theorem or global averaging.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Angle Embedding Initialization
comments.quote: assume that $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{jik}$ contain no information about the angles $\angle(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_k)$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the authors state that the initial angle embeddings contain no angle information. However, Equation 6 show $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{jik}$ being updated using $\angle(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_k)$. If the goal of the network is to be invariant to transformations of the coordinate embeddings, the 'geometric' angle must be the primary input. It is initially unclear to the reader whether $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{jik}$ is a learnable latent vector (like $\mathbf{h}$) or a geometric attribute. While Equation 6 clarifies that the geometric angle is used in the update, the prose 'assume they contain no information' suggests a cold-start, but fails to explicitly define how the initial value is set, which is critical for reproducibility.

comments.title: Unspecified Parameter in Conformal Equivariant Map
comments.quote: $$ \mathbf{x}_{i}^{+} = \mathbf{x}_{i} + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_{i}} a_{ji}(\mathbf{x}_{i} - \mathbf{x}_{j}) \quad (3) $$
comments.explanation: Equation 3 is presented as a map to achieve equivariance to the conformal orthogonal group. The authors state $a_{ji}$ could be a parametric function of other graph attributes. However, for this update to be equivariant to translations and rotations, $a_{ji}$ itself must be an invariant scalar. If $a_{ji}$ is learned as $\phi_x(\mathbf{e}_{ji})$, and $\mathbf{e}_{ji}$ is distance-invariant, the derivation holds. However, the paper lacks a formal statement or proof in the main body ensuring that $a_{ji}$ is constrained to be an invariant scalar, which is a key requirement for the equivariance claim to hold. A reader might incorrectly implement $a_{ji}$ as a vector-valued function.

comments.title: Inconsistency regarding Non-Orthogonal Generalization
comments.quote: The AGN... perfectly generalises to unseen polytopes whose neither angles nor edge lengths have been preserved (due to the non-orthogonality of $A$). This is probably due to the networks having learnt to distinguish polytopes based on the sum (or mean) of their angles which is preserved under non-orthogonal transformations due to the Gram–Euler theorem.
comments.explanation: This claim is technically questionable. The Gram-Euler theorem concerns the solid angles of a polytope (or sums of angles in specific geometries), but a general non-orthogonal transformation (a general linear map $A$) does *not* preserve arbitrary angles between rays. If the transformation $A$ is not conformal (i.e., not a scalar multiple of an orthogonal matrix), the angles $\angle(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_k)$ change. The authors admit that individual angles are not preserved, yet claim 'perfect generalization'. If the network is built to be invariant to the angles provided at the input, but those angles change during the test set transformation, the invariance property of the AGN should actually prevent it from generalizing unless the 'angle sums' mentioned are indeed constant across the transformation. This implies the model might be relying on global topological properties rather than the specific geometric equivariance built into the layers, potentially overstating the utility of the AGN architecture itself for non-conformal maps.

comments.title: Unclear Aggregation Indices in AGN
comments.quote: $$ \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{jik}^{+} = \phi_{\alpha}\left(\mathbf{h}_{j}, \mathbf{h}_{i}, \mathbf{h}_{k}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{jik}, \angle\left(\mathbf{x}_{j}, \mathbf{x}_{i}, \mathbf{x}_{k}\right), \mathbf{u}\right) \quad (6) $$
comments.explanation: In Equation 6, the node attributes $\mathbf{h}_j, \mathbf{h}_i, \mathbf{h}_k$ are used to update the angle embedding. While this is logically sound for a triplet, the following updates for $\mathbf{h}^{+}_i$ and $\mathbf{e}^{+}_{ji}$ require aggregation over the set of angles $A_i$. The paper uses $\rho_{\alpha \to h}$, but the exact indices are slightly ambiguous (e.g., does it aggregate all triplets where $i$ is the vertex, or where $i$ is any member?). Figure 2c helps, but the formal definition of $A_i$ as 'ordered couples ... whose vertex is node $i$' is slightly underspecified regarding whether $(j, i, k)$ and $(k, i, j)$ are treated as distinct or identical, which affects the scaling of the aggregation.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a thoughtful and well-motivated extension of graph neural networks with strong theoretical grounding in group theory and geometric deep learning. The core ideas—distance and angle preserving maps for equivariant modeling—are conceptually sound and aligned with recent advances in geometric deep learning. The architectures (DGN and AGN) are clearly described, and the experimental validation on synthetic polytopes convincingly demonstrates their strength in high-symmetry settings. However, there are several technical concerns around notation consistency, definition clarity, and justification of claims, particularly regarding the extension to conformal invariance and the role of non-orthogonal transformations. While these issues do not invalidate the main contributions, they affect the precision and reproducibility of the proposed methods. The paper would benefit from tighter formal definitions and more careful alignment between text, equations, and claims.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of angle preservation definition in AGN
comments.quote: We say that ψ is a relative angle preserving map if ∠( x[+] j , x[+] i , x[+] k ) = ∠( y j[+] , y i[+] , y k[+] ) , ∀(j, i, k) ∈A.
comments.explanation: The definition states that ψ preserves angles between triples in the output coordinates, but earlier in the same section, it is assumed that GX and GY already have identical angles. The concern is whether ψ is required to preserve angles only when input angles are equal, or always. This is critical: if ψ must preserve angles globally, its functional form is severely constrained (e.g., conformal maps). But the paper later suggests simple parametric forms like equation 3, which are not generally angle-preserving. Context from Section 3.3 suggests the intent is that ψ preserves angles only when the input embeddings GX and GY differ by a coordinate transformation preserving angles. However, this conditional aspect is not stated in the definition. The definition should clarify that ψ is required to preserve relative angles only when the input coordinate systems are related by an angle-preserving map, not that ψ is an angle-preserving function in general. As written, it could mislead a reader into thinking any ψ satisfying the condition is a conformal map, which is not the case.

comments.title: Missing functional form and parameters in ψ[x] definition
comments.quote:
A possible map ψ is defined by 

with aji possibly being a parametric function of other graph attributes.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'equation 3' as defining a possible map ψ, but no equation 3 is visible in the provided text. This appears to be a forward reference or OCR loss. The sentence implies that ψ has the form ψ(i, GX) = sum_{j∈Ni} a_ji (x_j − x_i) + b_i, but this formula is never explicitly given. While such a linear combination is standard in GNNs, the lack of a precise definition makes it impossible to verify whether this map truly preserves relative distances or angles as claimed. The paper relies on this form in both DGN and AGN to achieve equivariance, but without seeing the actual equation, one cannot assess whether it satisfies the definitions. For reproducibility, the functional form of ψ must be explicitly stated. Given the centrality of ψ[x] to both architectures, the omission constitutes a real gap.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about Gram–Euler theorem preserving sum of angles
comments.quote: This is probably due to the networks having learnt to distinguish polytopes based on the sum (or mean) of their angles which is preserved under nonorthogonal transformations due to the Gram–Euler theorem.
comments.explanation: The paper attributes unexpected generalization under non-orthogonal transformations to the sum of angles being preserved by the Gram–Euler theorem. However, the Gram–Euler theorem relates to the alternating sum of the number of faces of different dimensions in convex polytopes, not the sum of angles. The sum of angles at vertices is not invariant under arbitrary linear transformations. For example, stretching a regular tetrahedron will change internal angles. The claim that angle sums are preserved under non-orthogonal A is incorrect. While it's possible the networks exploit some higher-order combinatorial-geometric invariant, attributing it to the Gram–Euler theorem is misleading. This is more than a minor misattribution—it suggests a mathematical justification that does not hold. The phenomenon may still be real (perhaps due to normalization or other factors), but the explanation is faulty.

comments.title: Conflation of conformal group and conformal orthogonal group
comments.quote: Then, we introduce angle attributes to build equivariance to any angle preserving transformation - thus, to the conformal group.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that the AGN is equivariant to the conformal group Conf(R^n,0), but this is too strong. The conformal group in dimension n ≥ 3 consists of compositions of translations, rotations, dilations, and inversions (Möbius transformations). Inversions are nonlinear and not globally defined (due to poles), and the paper provides no mechanism to handle them. The update rule for node coordinates (ψ[x]) is linear in relative coordinates (if equation 3 is the standard weighted sum), which cannot represent inversions. The earlier definition of the 'conformal orthogonal group' as orthogonal transformations (including reflections) plus dilations is not standard—orthogonal + dilation gives similarities, which are a subgroup of the conformal group, but not the full group. So the AGN may be invariant to the similarity group (Euclidean + dilation), but not the full conformal group. This overstatement affects the paper's main claim of extending beyond known architectures. The distinction matters because true conformal equivariance requires handling inversions, which the architecture does not appear to do.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of relative distance preserving map
comments.quote: We say that ψ is a relative distance preserving map if ∥ x[+] i − x[+] j ∥[2] = ∥ y i[+] − y j[+] ∥[2] , ∀(i, j) ∈E.
comments.explanation: The definition requires that the updated coordinates preserve the relative distances between GX and GY, but it does not specify that the transformation from x to x[+] must preserve distances within a single graph. The condition as written only compares two different graphs GX and GY. This could be interpreted as ψ needing to preserve the distance between corresponding node pairs across transformed versions, not that it preserves edge lengths in the same graph. However, for equivariance, what's needed is that if GX and GY are related by a distance-preserving transformation, then so are the updated embeddings. The definition is technically correct in that context, but the term 'relative distance preserving map' is ambiguous—it sounds like a map that preserves distances in a single graph, but it's actually about consistency across related graphs. A clearer phrasing would be 'consistent under distance-preserving coordinate transformations'. The current wording could mislead a reader into thinking the map itself is an isometry.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for node coordinates and features in equations
comments.quote: In equation (5): e[+] ji = φ[e] (hj , hi, eji, u), h[+] i = φ[h] (hi, e[+] j∈Ni , u), x[+] i = ψ[x] (xj , j ∈ Ni ∪ {i}, u)
comments.explanation: The paper states that node features are v_i = [x_i, h_i], separating coordinates from other features. However, in the update for x[+]_i, only x_j inputs are mentioned, not h_j. But in the description of ψ[x], it's implied that a_ji can depend on other attributes like e_ji. This suggests that ψ[x] may actually depend on h_j or e_ji indirectly through a_ji, but the functional arguments of ψ[x] are written only as coordinates and u. This is a notation inconsistency: either ψ[x] must take additional arguments, or the dependence must be encoded through the form of the weights a_ji which are themselves outputs of a neural network. The paper later mentions a_ji as a parametric function of e_ji, but this is not reflected in the argument list of ψ[x]. The current notation underspecifies the actual implementation and could mislead about input dependencies.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents two novel graph network architectures (DGN and AGN) designed to be equivariant to distance and angle preserving transformations. The motivation is clear and the theoretical framework is well-grounded in group theory. The experiments demonstrate impressive data efficiency on synthetic datasets with symmetries and appropriately show limitations on datasets without symmetries. However, there are several issues with mathematical rigor and clarity that need addressing, particularly around the formal definitions and key invariance claims.

comments.title: Incomplete equivariance definition
comments.quote: Then, the linear map Φ : _X → Y_ , _Y ⊆_ R _[n]_ , is equivariant to _G_ if _∀g ∈ G_ , _∃ϕ[′] g_[:] _[ Y][→][Y]_[such that]
comments.explanation: The definition of equivariance appears to be cut off mid-sentence. The standard definition requires that Φ(φ_g(x)) = φ'_g(Φ(x)) for all g ∈ G and x ∈ X. Without completing this definition, the formal foundation for the rest of the paper is weakened. While this might be an OCR error, it's a critical definition that should be complete.

comments.title: Confusing statement about E(n) invariant functions
comments.quote: A function that is invariant under _E_ ( _n_ ) is _∥_ **x** _i −_ **x** _j∥_ 2[2][, for any] **[ x]** _[i][,]_ **[ x]** _[j][∈]_[E] _[n]_[ (see] Appendix A for a proof).
comments.explanation: This statement is mathematically imprecise. The norm ∥x_i - x_j∥² is not a function but a scalar value. The authors likely meant that any function of the form f(∥x_i - x_j∥²) would be invariant under E(n), but the phrasing suggests the norm itself is the invariant function, which is incorrect. This could confuse readers about what constitutes an invariant function.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for DGN invariance
comments.quote: Because of the way in which node coordinates are processed to update edge and global embeddings ( _i.e._ , only through their relative distances), it can be easily seen that **e**[+] _ji_[,] **[h]**[+] _i_[and] **[u]**[+][are,][by][con-] struction, invariant to any transformation of the input coordinates that locally maintains their relative distances along the edges defined by the graph structure (a notable example being the one of Euclidean transformations; see Appendix D for a proof).
comments.explanation: The claim that invariance can be "easily seen" is insufficient for a key property of the proposed architecture. While the authors reference Appendix D for a proof, such a fundamental claim should have at least a sketch of the justification in the main text. The invariance property is central to the contribution, so readers shouldn't need to consult an appendix to understand why it holds.

comments.title: Speculative explanation for AGN's unexpected generalization
comments.quote: This is probably due to the networks having learnt to distinguish polytopes based on the sum (or mean) of their angles which is preserved under nonorthogonal transformations due to the Gram–Euler theorem.
comments.explanation: The explanation for AGN's perfect generalization to non-orthogonal transformations seems speculative. The Gram-Euler theorem doesn't guarantee that the sum of angles is preserved under arbitrary non-orthogonal transformations. This claim needs either a mathematical proof or empirical verification (e.g., checking if the angle sums are indeed preserved in the transformed test data). Without proper justification, this could mislead readers about the actual mechanism.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated approach to building graph neural networks that are equivariant to distance-preserving (DGN) and angle-preserving (AGN) transformations. Comparing these architectures to existing SE(3) and E(n) equivariant models is intellectually coherent. However, the rigor of the mathematical definitions and notation in the provided text appears to have suffered significantly during the OCR process. Key equations and aggregate update rules are often corrupted or illegible, making the formal methodology difficult to verify. While the conceptual framework is sound, the presentation lacks the necessary precision for the reader to fully reconstruct the model operations.

comments.title: Corrupted update function definitions
comments.quote: where $\phi_e : \mathbb{R}^{2n_v + n_e + n_u} \to \mathbb{R}^{n_e} , \phi_v : \mathbb{R}^{n_v + n_e + n_u} \to \mathbb{R}^{n_v} , \phi_u : \mathbb{R}^{n_v + n_e + n_u} \to \mathbb{R}^{n_u}$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the domains of the update functions ($\phi_e, \phi_v, \phi_u$) are mathematically inconsistent with standard graph network definitions. For example, $\phi_v$ depends on edge and global attributes, but the input dimension $\mathbb{R}^{n_v + n_e + n_u}$ implies it takes a single node feature vector, one edge vector, and one global vector; it fails to account for the aggregation of multiple incident edges or the variable-length nature of the neighborhood. While this is likely an issue with the OCR transcription, it renders the formal definition of the GN block opaque.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the conformal group
comments.quote: Then, a conformal transformation $\phi$ satisfies... (equations missing)
comments.explanation: The text references a formal condition for conformal transformations in Section 2.1, but the actual mathematical definition/equation is missing from the provided text following the sentence describing the triple of vectors. This makes it difficult to assess the exact criteria for the AGN block's equivariance.

comments.title: Missing definition of the angle embedding update
comments.quote: where $\phi_\alpha , \phi_e , \phi_h , \psi_x , \phi_u$ are the update functions, and $\rho_{e\to h} , \rho_{\alpha\to h} , \rho_{h\to u} , \rho_{e\to u} , \rho_{\alpha\to u}$ are the aggregation functions
comments.explanation: Section 3.3 refers to update functions defined in equation 6, but the actual equation 6 is not provided in the text. Furthermore, the footnote 1 attempts to describe the domain of $\phi_\alpha$ but the OCR output is garbled as '$\phi_\alpha : \mathbb{R}^{3 n_h + n_\alpha + 1 + n_u}$'. Given the input needs, the logic for how $\phi_\alpha$ operates on triples is not explicitly stated, leaving the implementation underspecified.

comments.title: Use of undefined variable in scaling factor
comments.quote: Let $\gamma = \alpha / \max_{(i,j) \in E} \| \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \|$ for some $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$
comments.explanation: The parameter $\alpha$ is introduced in the definition of the scale factor $\gamma$ in Section 4.2. However, this $\alpha$ is not previously defined in the context of the DGN scaling layer, and it conflicts with the usage of $\alpha_{jik}$ as the angle embedding defined in Section 3.3. It is unclear if this is a hyperparameter, a learnable parameter, or a specific constant.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a theoretically motivated extension of graph neural networks that decouple coordinate updates from feature updates to enforce equivariance/invariance under distance- and angle-preserving transformations. The core idea is sound and aligns with recent trends in geometric deep learning. However, the manuscript suffers from critical omissions in its mathematical formulation: the core update equations are missing, the formal condition for conformal transformations is absent, and several theoretical claims (particularly regarding inversion singularities and local symmetry operationalization) lack necessary mathematical grounding or boundary condition specifications. Additionally, the interpretation of empirical results contains a speculative claim that conflicts with standard linear algebra properties. Addressing these gaps is essential for reproducibility and for validating the claimed symmetry guarantees.

comments.title: Missing core update equations for GN, DGN, and AGN blocks
comments.quote:
A general standard graph network block can be defined in terms of edge, node and global updates as 

where _φ[e]_ : R[2] _[n][v]_[+] _[n][e]_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] e_[+] , _φ[v]_ : R _[n][v]_[+] _[n] e_[+][+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] v_[+] , _φ[u]_ : R _[n] v_[+][+] _[n]_[+] _e_[+] _[n][u] →_ R _[n] u_[+] are update functions...
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused because the text explicitly references equations 4, 5, and 6 to define the standard Graph Network (GN), Distance Preserving Graph Network (DGN), and Angle Preserving Graph Network (AGN) blocks, but the algebraic update rules are completely absent from the provided manuscript. I checked Figures 1 and 2 to see if the update rules were visualized in the architecture diagrams, but those figures only show high-level data flow and aggregation pathways without explicit formulas. Without the actual functional forms of _ψ^[x]_, _φ^[α]_, _ρ^[e→v]_, etc., it is impossible to verify the invariance claims or reproduce the models. This is a major methodological gap.

comments.title: Missing mathematical condition for conformal transformations
comments.quote:
Then, a conformal transformation _ϕ_ satisfies 

By definition, the transformations of the conformal group include translations, rotations, reflections (which collectively form the Euclidean group _E_ ( _n_ ), as well as dilations, inversions and other features.
comments.explanation: I initially noticed a blank line where the defining equation for conformal transformations should be. I checked the surrounding text to see if the condition was described verbally or deferred to an appendix, but the paragraph immediately jumps to 'By definition, the transformations...'. The standard condition (typically expressed via the Jacobian satisfying _J^⊤ J = λ(x) I_ or the angle preservation identity) is entirely omitted. While experts might infer the intended definition, its absence breaks the formal progression and leaves the precise mathematical scope of the AGN's equivariance claim ambiguous.

comments.title: Unaddressed singularity in inversion transformations
comments.quote: With the AGN, we can relax this condition and be invariant under the full conformal group.
comments.explanation: The authors claim the AGN is invariant under the full conformal group, which mathematically includes inversions of the form _ϕ(x) = x / ‖x‖²_. I initially wondered if the architecture implicitly handles this via coordinate normalization, but the model uses raw coordinates in distance/angle computations and message passing. Inversions are singular at the origin, causing coordinates to blow up when points approach _x=0_. No regularization, clamping strategy, or coordinate shifting is described. Without a mechanism to handle this singularity, the claimed invariance is theoretically sound but practically unstable or undefined for real-world datasets.

comments.title: Vague operationalization of local symmetries and subgraph selection
comments.quote: Namely, the DGN and AGN preserve local distances and angles respectively _where a suitable subgraph must be defined_ .
comments.explanation: I was initially intrigued by the claim of local symmetry handling, as it extends global equivariance to flexible domains. However, I checked the manuscript for how this 'suitable subgraph' is determined or updated, and found no concrete specification. It is unclear whether the subgraph is statically partitioned, dynamically learned via attention masks, or defined by the existing edge set _E_. Furthermore, standard message-passing GNNs assume a fixed adjacency structure during a forward/backward pass; switching subgraphs would require non-standard topological changes. The methodological details are insufficient for implementation.

comments.title: Speculative and mathematically inconsistent explanation for non-orthogonal generalization
comments.quote: This is probably due to the networks having learnt to distinguish polytopes based on the sum (or mean) of their angles which is preserved under nonorthogonal transformations due to the Gram–Euler theorem.
comments.explanation: I initially assumed this referred to a known geometric invariant, but upon checking the Gram–Euler theorem, I found it relates edge lengths and angles in a simplex (e.g., via Cayley-Menger determinants), not angle preservation under linear maps. By definition, non-orthogonal transformations distort angles; the sum or mean of angles is not preserved under general non-orthogonal linear maps _A_ where _A^⊤ A ≠ I_. The claim contradicts basic linear algebra and lacks derivation or citation. This weakens the interpretation of Table 1's results and suggests the network may be learning a different, unexamined heuristic rather than strictly exploiting the stated symmetries.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces two interesting graph neural network architectures (DGN and AGN) that extend group equivariance to distance- and angle-preserving transformations, including the conformal group. This is a timely and relevant contribution to geometric deep learning. However, the paper contains significant issues that undermine its experimental validation and theoretical claims. Specifically, the experimental analysis relies on a mathematically incorrect justification for why the AGN generalizes to non-orthogonal transforms, and the reported results contradict the paper's own explicit claims of 'perfect' generalization. Furthermore, the architectural definitions for the DGN and AGN blocks are underspecified; they repeat the generic GNN equations without clarifying how node features are updated to maintain invariance when coordinates are transformed, creating a potential implementation inconsistency. Addressing these issues is essential before the paper can be accepted.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes two novel graph network architectures (DGN and AGN) aimed at achieving equivariance to distance- and angle-preserving transformations, respectively, including subgroups of the Euclidean and conformal groups. The ideas are interesting and the experimental results on synthetic polytopes demonstrate clear benefits of equivariance. However, several inconsistencies between the textual equations and the architectural diagrams, as well as questionable claims about invariance under non-orthogonal transformations, reduce clarity and trustworthiness. The core contributions are promising, but the presentation needs careful revision.

comments.title: Inconsistency between equations and diagrams for DGN and AGN
comments.explanation: In the DGN block (Equation 5), the node and global updates receive only edge-aggregated messages and previous attributes. However, Figure 2(b) shows additional connections from ψ^x through ρ^{x→h} and ρ^{x→u} into the φ^h and φ^u updates. The same discrepancy appears for the AGN block (Equation 6 and Figure 2(c)). The text does not resolve this, leaving ambiguity about whether coordinate-derived information is used in these updates. This mismatch makes it difficult for a reader to reproduce the exact architecture.

comments.title: Typo in coordinate update: uses G_X' instead of G_X
comments.quote: x'_i = ψ^x(i, G_X')
comments.explanation: The definition of a relative distance preserving map (and the earlier discussion) specifies that ψ takes the original graph G_X as argument. Using G_X' (the updated graph) would create a circular dependency since x'_i depends on itself. The intended symbol is almost certainly G_X. This typo appears in both Equation 5 and Equation 6.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated explanation for perfect test accuracy under non-orthogonal transformations
comments.quote: This is probably due to the networks having learnt to distinguish polytopes based on the sum (or mean) of their angles which is preserved under nonorthogonal transformations due to the Gram–Euler theorem.
comments.explanation: The AGN achieves 100% test accuracy even when the test set applies non-orthogonal linear transformations that do not preserve angles (and that the paper says change both distances and angles). The authors attribute this to an invariant sum of angles, citing the Gram–Euler theorem without any reference or explanation. It is not clear how a theorem about polyhedral angles (if that is what is referenced) would guarantee invariance under arbitrary linear transformations of coordinates. The claim appears speculative and lacks sufficient justification, potentially misleading readers about the actual invariance properties of the model.
